title: Macaroni pie
subtitle: Trinidadian baked macaroni set firm with egg and cheddar
excerpt: Baked macaroni and cheese in the Trinidadian style: set firm with eggs so it slices into clean squares, seasoned with scotch bonnet, onion, and thyme. A fixture at every Trinidadian Sunday table.
sourceNotes: Trinidadian macaroni pie differs from American mac and cheese in that eggs are added to the cheese sauce before baking, which sets the dish firm enough to cut into squares. It is baked in a deep rectangular tin rather than a gratin dish. The result is closer to a baked pasta frittata than a loose mac and cheese. Traditional seasoning includes scotch bonnet, onion, thyme, and mustard, the mustard is standard in Trinidadian cooking for this dish. Evaporated milk is often used in place of whole milk for a slightly richer, sweeter result.

content.text: Trinidadian macaroni pie is not the same as mac and cheese, despite the identical ingredients. The eggs set the dish firm during baking, which means it can be sliced into squares and served as a side at a table rather than scooped from a dish. It is eaten alongside pelau, stewed chicken, and callaloo at every Sunday gathering and every party. It is also, by any measure, very good.

The seasoning is important: scotch bonnet, onion, thyme, and mustard are standard. The mustard sharpens the cheddar flavour and prevents the dish from being too rich. A generous amount of cheese, used both in the sauce and scattered on top, gives a golden, slightly crisp surface.

items.name: Whole milk
items.prepNote: or evaporated milk for a richer result

content.text: Cook the {{pasta-dried}} of pasta in well-salted boiling water for 2 minutes less than the packet instructions. It should be just underdone since it will finish in the oven. Drain and rinse briefly with cold water to stop cooking.

Preheat the oven to 180°C fan. In a large bowl, whisk together the {{eggs}} eggs, {{whole-milk}} of milk, {{mustard-powder}} of mustard powder, {{sea-salt-fine}} of salt, {{onion}} onion, {{scotch-bonnet}} scotch bonnet (if using), and {{thyme-fresh}} of fresh thyme. Stir in three-quarters of the {{cheddar}} of grated cheddar. Add the drained pasta and mix well to combine.

Grease a deep rectangular baking tin with the {{unsalted-butter}} of butter. Pour in the pasta mixture and spread level. Scatter the remaining cheddar over the top. Bake for 40 to 45 minutes until golden and set, a knife inserted in the centre should come out clean. Leave to rest for 10 minutes before cutting into squares.

items.fix: The mixture must be fully set before removing. Cover with foil and continue baking for 10 to 15 more minutes. A deep tin needs longer; next time use a shallower tin.
items.cause: Too much milk; tin too deep; oven not hot enough

items.fix: Cover with foil after 25 minutes and continue until set. Reduce oven to 170°C fan if this happens.
items.cause: Oven too hot; cheese on top too thick

items.fix: Undercook the pasta by at least 2 minutes compared to the packet instructions. It continues cooking in the oven.
Use a mature cheddar. Season the pasta water generously. The mustard powder is essential, don't skip it.

content.text: Macaroni pie is the side dish that no Trinidadian Sunday table is complete without. It sits alongside pelau, stewed chicken or curry, and callaloo: the starch that absorbs the sauce and rounds out the meal. The name 'pie' is accurate in the Trinidadian sense of something baked in a dish and served in portions, not quite a pastry pie, but a distinct, sliceable thing. Leftovers are eaten cold the next day or warmed in the oven, and are often better than the fresh version.
